strategy_summary: From towering trees to minute microalgae, plants provide virtually all living things with the food they need by capturing energy from sunlight and transforming it into sugars and other nourishing molecules. Plants that grow in wet places can use water to stay cool as they soak up the sun’s rays.
mechanism:
The leaf windows of Haworthia truncata work like natural skylights. They are made of translucent tissue at the top of each thick, buried leaf. While the windows themselves don’t photosynthesize, they allow sunlight to pass into the hidden photosynthetic tissues deeper inside the plant. Inside the leaf, light enters through the window and diffuses through layers of clear, water-storing cells, eventually reaching the chloroplast-rich zones that perform photosynthesis .

The tip of Fenestraria aurantiaca’s stem is clear, allowing sunlight to pass through it into the stem below. And like the lens of your eye, the clear part is somewhat selective about what kind of light gets through. Its waxy surface filters out the highest-energy light, allowing only about 10 percent of the total light into the plant, preventing the insides from overheating. The lens-like tip is also rounded on top. The curved shape directs the light that hits its surface inward, toward the center of the stem. It also keeps sand from piling up and obstructing the window.

The internal layout of these leaves is adapted to make the most of the light that filters in. Beneath the window layer, there’s a dome-like zone of translucent tissue that scatters light sideways and downward, spreading it across a broader area. This reduces hot spots and increases the efficiency of light use by buried chloroplast s. Light sensors inside the leaves show that light intensity and quality deep in the leaf closely match those just beneath the window surface, confirming that these windows successfully deliver usable light.

This strategy reveals the possibility of harvesting light in ways that are spatially decoupled from exposure. It suggests that illumination and environmental vulnerability need not go hand in hand. Systems can be designed to gather light above while shielding sensitive components below—allowing for function without direct exposure. This is especially relevant in contexts where excess heat, dryness, or damage from light would be detrimental, yet the benefits of photosynthetically active light—or its analogs in technology—are still desired.

The method for vertically transmitting light is used to some extent to bring light into buildings through tubes running from the roof to the occupied space. This technology could be dramatically enhanced by taking the window plant’s next step of using water or another substance that bends light to direct beams in the direction they’re needed most. It also can provide inspiration for designing solar photovoltaic installations that are less susceptible to overheating and better able to focus light on electricity-generating structures. It might even be used to design handheld electronics that are easier to view in direct sunlight by channeling the light into features that can illuminate the device, countering the glare.
